review_text: When it comes to Chaplins romance and balancing between comedy and tragedy, no other film hits as hard as this one.

There's no denying the groundwork laid here for the comedy genre as a whole, and that is no easy feat, making it Chaplin's well-deserved crowning achievement at the time. Yet, the very same comedic effect does seem to feel very dated in its years, mostly in its slapstick and over-the-top approach that did not age all that well.
The greatest romantic comedy ever made!
The film is beautiful and so simple.
4/4 Thee romantic comedy that's also one of the ten best American Films of all time. It quite possibly has the greatest ending to any movie in history.
One of the most beautiful movies you can watch. Definitely must-see.
Timeless classic. One of the most acclaimed Chaplin pictures. Beautiful story with all the charm only Charlot's films have.
What can you say? One of the best from one of the best. When a silent film can hold your attention and make you laugh almost a hundred years after release that's all you need to know.

The plot itself is not intricate, and almost just like in every comedy, it mostly serves to deliver Tramp to humorous set pieces. Even if you haven't seen it, chances are you've heard about the ending. It's considered one of the most perfect endings in cinema and one of the greatest scenes in Chaplin movies.
Cute but unremarkable.
Charlie Chaplin's ultimately refined emotional and comedic masterpiece that proves just how absolutely loveable a character like the Tramp is.
A thing of astonishing beauty. As great as so much of Chaplin's filmography is, I don't think he ever did anything as perfect as the final few minutes of this movie.
A masterpiece. A must see.

Ano passado eu tinha uma prova de literatura do filme e quando eu fui ver o filme pensei que seria sem graça mas eu estava enganado e um filme foda uma comédia um pouco forsada mas eu ri em 90% do filme o final e lindo merece um remeik
This is another classic by the legend. City Lights is full of great slap stick comedy that still holds up surprisingly well. It also has some heartfelt moments that feel very real.

This is my first Chaplin movie, and I'm blown away. I was laughing throughout, but the boxing scene was so hilarious, I couldn't stop laughing. The ending was moving without being maudlin. It really was a great film.
Heartbreakingly funny and sobering at the same time, Charlie Chaplin's masterpiece is unparalleled except for possibly some of his other works.
Simply beautiful and one of Chaplin's best.

City Lights is a classic motion picture. You can tell that Charlie Chaplin poured his heart and soul into this film. In a time when talking pictures were becoming all the rage, he fought to keep this film silent, and I think that's one of the reasons it stands out among the rest. The story may be considered silly or simple to the modern viewer, but this film has humor, it has heart, and it has drama. Even the acting, while intentionally hokey most of the time, brings real emotion in the end. City Lights is more than just a spectacularly choreographed boxing scene, it's a masterpiece of silent cinema.

The scenes that make the movie are when the Tramp finds himself in the locker room of the boxing gym and in the actual boxing ring. The musical score, directing, and physical humor is spot on, along with when he's at the dinner dance party with his new drunk delirious "pal"! This is the 3rd film of Chaplin's I've seen and I don't regularly rate movies higher than an 8 on IMDb but there you have it. This is the kind of movie that's perfect to come out and give people laughs during a historic age of economic depression in America. I'm very glad I watched this, Chaplin had such talent in his films and for filmmaking!

One of the most famous and best work of Charles Chaplin that has such profound heart and joy as it does comedy. This is another film that is a pleasure to watch and is timeless in every possible way. As the talkies were exploding in Hollywood and nearly everyone was making the switch, Chaplin was nearly done with City Lights and after great debate decided to finish it. Thank God he did though, as this is one of his greatest achievements, arguably his best, and a masterpiece of the Silent era! City Lights delivers in all the key areas and provides Chaplin's usual touch of humanity and his loveable demeanor coupled with his impeccable timing and hilarious slapstick. Currently sitting at #11 on the current American Film Institute Top 100 Films of the past 100 years, City Lights earns that spot by leaps and bounds. What an amazing work by one of film's most talented Director/Actors. If you haven't seen it you are missing out on a gem and need to do so as soon as possible!

I agree that this is Chaplin's best performance, but I don't think it's his best movie. The third act, particularly the boxing scene, was flawless, but for more than half the movie I felt the scenes weren't blending into each other very well and (especially in the first act) he was milking the decent jokes through bald repetition. The length of the boxing sequence really saves the movie as that whole part is perfect from start to finish and it taking up a significant amount of the runtime was good. It does make it rather difficult to justify AFI naming this the #1 rom-com though as the whole thing is like 5% romance at best.
The boxing scene is golden. The end is eloquently moving.

For adults who want to show children a purer form of comedy, City Lights is your movie. This sweet but comic slapstick classic shows Chaplin deceiving a blind flower girl to gain her love. It is a fun movie and a valuable one. If you've never seen a silent film, this is the one to see. It teaches the lessons that appearances can be deceptive and a man with nothing to his name can be as honorable as any millionaire. As an actor, Chaplin is a marvel, conveying astonishing emotional depth through the silent antics of his little tramp. As a director, he demonstrates an impeccable sense of timing with difficult, gracefully choreographed stunts. The entire family may bust a collective gut watching him skitter across a slippery dance floor, or outmaneuver an imposing boxing opponent, but then be rendered speechless by the poignant ending. To blink while watching is to miss something. Like Modern Times, another masterpiece, this film touches on some sensitive issues but doesn't become weighed down by them. A morose drunk man makes repeated suicide attempts, although the audience never feels the danger. This was a thumb in the eye to talkies by Chaplin and is perhaps his best film ever.
Maravilhoso, sensível e cativante.

The importance of Charlie Chaplin films cannot be overstated, and City Lights always pops up whenever you discuss his best. A classic blend of slapstick and pathos, the kind that The Tramp character was always associated with, I did laugh a number of times, but I also spent significant periods waiting for something significant to happen. It's obvious how talented Chaplin was, and he dedicated himself fully to his craft, and while the material he comes up with is usually amusing, he seems to have trouble filling out an entire film. Some scenes, including the prelude to the boxing match, and the match itself, go on for much longer than they should, and just start repeating the same jokes to diminishing effect. It's a fun story, and The Tramp's hapless antics are always bolstered by the fact that he is, in essence, always trying to do good, never hurts anyone out of malice, and ultimately is just trying to get by in a difficult world. Maybe Chaplin's style of comedy isn't to my taste, but I certainly appreciate how much he did for the world of Cinema at large, and there's certainly a lot of elements to recommend. Not to everyone's taste, but it's still a landmark film that any movie buff should see at least once.
The film is unpredictible and funny, and the story is really nice. City Lights is a solid classic.
"You know what they say: love… is blind." — Tommy Wisseau, the Chaplin of bad movies
Amazing movie. Chaplin's masterpiece and the first film that really got me into films. Superb in every way.
I preferred the Gold Rush, but particularly the latter half of this film (from about the boxing scene on) hits that magic point of comedy and pathos that Chaplin did so well.

If you want a movie you can take apart piece by piece, this is the one. Every scene has meaning and evokes a wide range of emotions. City Lights has a couple of the funniest scenes ever shown on the motion-picture screen and, at the end, it gives us one of the movie's most heart-touching moments.
Slow and boring in places but there are the usual Chaplin highlights like the boxing fight and the love interest. Not one of my faves but has its moments.

After seeing a few Charlie Chaplin films, one thing I have discovered is that his movies combine heart, social commentary, and funny slapstick very well. "City Lights" is another one of his movies that is considered a definitive classic and it is easy to see why. The movie has many sweet and funny moments boosted by a strong overall theme. I would say out of the movies I have seen so far, my personal favorite is "Modern Times." Although at this point in time I have only seen three of them, so as I explore more classics, that could always change, I would say this movie is almost as good as that one. Silent films are hard for me to truly love, but I think the ones I like I can certainly appreciate as products of their time, and this is one of them.  Overall, this movie left me with an upbeat and positive feeling many movies are not able to pull off.
A really endearing film that I love

Whenever I read about silent films Charlie Chaplin always seemed to come up, and having never seen a silent film I thought that City Lights was a movie with a simplistic plot that was executed in the best way possible. The end scene is probably one of the most memorable in all of cinema.

Real Rating: 83.5/100 This is my 3rd favourite film of Chaplin's. Modern Times and the Great Dictator, rank higher for me personally. Though to the man himself, this is his favourite film and Welles, Kubrick, Tarkovsky and Woody Allen agree. It is strange though that Chaplin's 3 greatest works came after he was deemed obsolete, but being the artistic Luddite, he was. He pushed forward and did not allow modern technology to steal his craftsmanship. In a way, you must applaud that, but at the same time from a critical analysis standpoint, you must also detract marks for it. For is it genius? Or a has-been doing their best to cling on to a world that has moved beyond them? BE: With a Chaplin film you have to ask yourself can you really comment on the directing. Given that he does a large percentage of the workload himself, it would be like applauding Michelangelo's overseeing of the Sistine Chapel rather than the painting itself. The editing is decent, and the transitions move swiftly and give a sense of speed and the story with what limited cinematography we have for the time. W: The writing is simple but effective. Some claim that the pathos as they put it is limiting the film and not allowing it to be as good as it can be. However, we have seen the Tramp 12 times prior to this, and this is the last outing. Giving this comedic character a sense of compassion and sympathy at this stage is where the story should progress. Rather than leaving it in the purely farcical realm. We have completeness and sadness that often appears in great comedy, and from the very first scene with the Tramp and the blind girl, we have a pleasant mix of longing with pratfall comedy. C: It is 1931, and Fritz Arno Wagner is continuing to put Hollywood cinematography to shame. Meanwhile, in Hollywood, Chaplin has brought along his old standby in Roland Totheroh, and he provides the standard for a Chaplin outing. The angles on some shots are nice, and some of the shadow work is pleasant, but nothing outstanding. A: At the beginning of the film, it says it is in pantomime, which implies over the top acting and with subtly and nuance removed from the piece. Which would be fine, save for the fact that it does have these things due to the previously mentioned pathos. Which forces me to be meaner on some of the more weak acting exhibited by the rest of the crew. Chaplin is excellent and very few people have such command of body language as he. The blind girl did not always feel blind, but we are still in the early days of cinema, so it is slightly excusable.  S: The opening of the film is annoying in terms of sound. We have mumbled audio from several characters, and then the Tramp appears and then silence. Which seems to imply yes films have dialogue now but let us enjoy this nostalgic throwback. Which is an excellent way of presenting the film, but in terms of audio quality, it is weak, and we could have had that same effect while presenting clear audio to the viewer. The rest is standard silent film fare. PD: The sets are sets of the era, and they took effort, but nothing stands out, especially when one knows what is coming in the 1930s in regards to set design. BA: Costume design is again very Hollywood and of the era. Nothing makes it feel all that special save for the Tramp himself. EVO: Thoroughly enjoying and while the comedy is not on par with the circus or modern times. It offers the viewer so much more and allows us to see how romantic films should be done. I kid you not. Look at most of the great romantic movies, and you will find that the romance in the film is left in the background and only adds to the overall work. Like a lovely sauce over a delicious meal. Rather than your standard romantic movie which is like eating a 15 kg bag of marshmallows in a single sitting.

A classic (many would say "the classic") Charlie Chaplin comedy-drama about a poor man in love with a blind flower girl, and her believing he's a millionaire. As always, it's impossible to peel your eyes away from Chaplin's performance, as the sweet, and slightly mischievous Little Tramp. A ton of heart and a good amount of drama, also in the form of his relationship with a suicidal drunkard millionaire who is only nice when he's drunk, in this movie makes it a great watch. With it not being 100% a silent movie, there are great musical-instrument related jokes the just add to Chaplin's famous physicality. Great performances all around in this lovely and delightful movie.
A chaplin classic, but not one of my favorites

Charlie Chaplin sports one of his greatest performances in City Lights. The film is still hilarious, (the scene at the dinner in particular), very creative (Chaplin is perhaps more expressive and charming in his silence than most modern actors are), but it's ending is still what stands out to me after all these years. It is definitely simple, but is beautifully sweet nonetheless.

Chaplin's physical comedy remains timeless, and is blended seamlessly into a story just as empathetic as that of The Kid. The boxing scene remains one of Chaplin's best from his entire career. (4.5/5)
Sometimes silent films can make me laugh harder than anything.

For those who know me can testify that I am NOT a fan of silent movies. I am super picky about them and it is very hard to get me to stay focused on watching one. Due to the fact the film is silent, I end up falling asleep because it is so quiet, and I get distracted easily. But, that is not the case with the film, "City Lights." City Lights probably has to be my favorite silent film I have seen. (Which is saying a lot). The characters and the plot of the movie was fantastic. My favorite characters in the film was Charlie Chaplin and the eccentric billionaire The main reason I even wanted to see the film is because of Charlie Chaplin. I love Charlie Chaplin. He made me laugh almost the entire movie. His character was funny, and he played the character of "The Tramp" beautifully. My other favorite character was the eccentric billionaire. He made me laugh the entire movie as well. More importantly the way Chaplin's character and the billionaire interactions were always so funny.

City Lights (1931) Director Charlie Chaplin City lights is one of the best Charlie Champlin films. Charlie is the director and main actor playing his famous Tramp role. The movie is charming and fun that is a textbook showcase of slapstick and excellent showcase of a silent movie movie. The slapstick is amazing and can be studied to see how to do proper slapstick. The boxing scene is iconic and plays out like a excelant choreographed dance. The opening scene is also a wonderful showcase of Champlin's skills in slapstick and is a fun way to start the film off and gives us the mood. The story as a whole is charming and funny. But also deals with some serious issues in a compelling way while not completely making fun of them, like suiside and being poor. The relationship between the Tramp and the drunk Millionaire, played by Harry Myers, is one way this film deals with the poor status of men. Tramp is very poor and he makes friends with a Millionaire after he says the mans live from suiside. The film shows us how even the man with a lot of money needs to be saved from taking his own life, which one would most likely only due if there are unhappy with life which also shows us how having money in this world isn't everything. Then there is the build up between the relationship of the Tramp and the Blind Girl, played by Virginia Cherrill, is cute and builds to an interaction that does not fail to spark multiple emotions. This is really thanks to the phenomenal acting done by Champlin and Cherrill. The intereaction im talking about is the ending when the Blind Girl finally sees the Tramp for the first time in the film when the whole film she believes the Tramp to be a rich man when he is not. The subtle change that Champlin has from excitement when he finally finds his love to worry and embarrassed when he realizes that she is actually seeing him. This is played wonderfully by Charrills character who has this slow realization of her own about Tramp and how he was the one who took care of her for so long. These two plays with our emotions; making us feel anxious for their first interaction, to embarrassed for Champlin, hopeful for their future, all in a matter of seconds with no dialogue and very little title cards. Along with the amazing acting the music also is just as amazing. It plays along with the story as a whole. Being upbeat and fun during the best slapstick parts of the movie. While also being sad and down during the times when the Tramp is down on his luck or feeling low. City Lights is an excellent film that plays with ideas of how you status in society or how much money you have doesn't make you a good or bad person. Or make your life any easier. It is classic ideals that Champlin showcases in his other films but this one is one of his masterpieces. City Lights might be Charlie Champlins best film of his career.

"City Lights" (1931) Directed by Charlie Chaplin. Cast: Charlie Chaplin, Virginia Cherrill, and Harry Myers. City lights came out at the end of an era as sound was being introduced in the next few years, It shows how good silent films can be with Charlie Chaplin going all in, in this hilarious comedy the slap stick comedy is so outrageous and goofy it still makes audiences laugh. For a comedy I was very surprised to find out that this movie had a romantic relationship between the Tramp (Charlie Chaplin) and Blind Girl (Virginia Cherrill). This film does so good with the characters, Chaplin and Cherrill have chemistry that makes you want them to end up with each other at the end of the movie. I was also very surprised with a slapstick comedy like this that it was able to have so much heart, that makes this film so much more than just random sequences with plenty of jokes, by the end we find out that it was a romantic story between a blind woman and the Tramp. This film Has so much going for it and still holds up to this day.

City Lights was my first Chaplin film I had ever seen, and one of the first silent films I have ever seen. Going into it, I honestly wasn't very hopeful as I had never seen myself enjoying a silent film as movies today rely so heavily on sound that I almost stubbornly didn't believe it could be down otherwise. Needless to say, I can happily admit that I was terribly wrong about silent films, City Lights captured me in a way I didn't realize ever could during a movie. The lack of audio almost brought me into the movie MORE because it then needed more of my attention. Chaplin's performance is nothing short of masterful. What is is capable of doing through actions is uncanny. His performance in this movie puts nearly all modern day acting to shame. The way Chaplin is able to grab at the audience's various emotions is so impressive. There were parts in this film where I laughed, where I smiled, where I felt sorry for the character, and where I felt awful for him. Modern day movies even struggle with that and the fact that Chaplin did this all while only using his face and actions leaves me speechless. Chaplin's way of moviemaking is so inspiring. I believe he revolutionized the business. Directly after watching City Lights all I wanted to do was go grab a camera and start shooting a silent film of my own. Overall, this movie blew me away. The fact that I walked into it disliking silent films, then walking out in love with it, I think that speaks massive volumes to the level of excellence this film has.

City Lights was overall a good movie for what was accessible to film makers at the time. Since movies were just being made at that time, there was no dialog, you really had to flex your creative mind to try and get your message out to the audience. This was utilized by Chaplin with both music, (non-diegetic) and his over the top actions he used. One example I heard when the soundtrack really set the tone of the scene was when Chaplin first realized the lady selling flowers was blind, he put two and two together and the music slowed, it turned almost empathetic and softer, so the audience would feel what Charlie felt in that scene. There was no need for words or any text cards. The actions also helped move the plot of the story, since there weren't going to be any jokes being told, again, this was Charlie really needing to think about how he was going to progress the story while also making the viewers laugh. So slapstick comedy was the answer to that, and it worked. Even today I still laugh at the silly gags he uses to entertain, it's simple and effective, people like seeing others get slightly hurt or someone making a dumb decision. But what really makes it believable and amazing was that these effects all had to be "real", there was no post production editing, no special effects to hide the stunt, the actors had to do it themselves, and I think everyone can agree that practical effects look better than any CGI effects. So in conclusion, Chaplin was able to make a timeless film with no real speaking, but told compelling story, all while making us laugh along the way and really look at the stunts differently.

While I knew who Charlie Chaplin was, City Lights was the first Chaplin movie I have ever watched. It did not disappoint. I found his style thoroughly entertaining throughout the movie and it constantly reminded me of recent comedy movies. It shows the profound impact Chaplin had on comedy. I think what stuck with me the most was how the wealthy man would treat the tramp, played by Chaplin, like a close friend he had known for years when he was drunk, but when he sobered up in the morning it was like he had never met Chaplin before. It was almost like Chaplin was his drunken mistake that he kept making, and I found this humorous because I could relate to it! It relates the whole idea that people do things when they're drunk that they'd never dream of doing when they're sober and Chaplin does an immense job of showing the confusion and regret the wealthy man feels, but then the repeated commitment to making that same mistake when he's drunk. Chaplin does such a great job at relating to his audience by conveying human emotion. His writing makes you feel the emotions of the characters in situations you might find yourself in and that is what makes it so funny. It's almost like "been there done that," and because it's so clear what the characters are feeling, you don't need many text cards. The lack of text was intentional because seeing these situations without context but still completely understanding the emotions and thoughts of his characters was funny in itself, and trying to make humor through conversations in silent movies takes away some of the humor as the audience has to read each text card. Chaplin understood this and did a fantastic job showing emotion that did not need context. This is what makes City Lights, and his other films, classics, not just in comedy, but in all cinema.

Its a great romance and comedy. It works for people who want to see a comedy or people who want to see a romance. The movie is absolutely great and it might even make you shed a tear. Which is the most impressive thing about this film. The fact that it works as a slapstick comedy and yet it is so satisfying. You need to watch it.

Day and night. Day and night. This is how the film travels making sure that both of them are present at once. City Lights Charlie Chaplin's boxing match is not a serious match. I know that's obvious. But it is not. Not expected. At the back your thoughts, you are always waiting to get things serious. Gritty. Bloody. Brutal. Intense. And it is these films that makes us expect these things from a genre as such. Not even touching the Rocky milestone, I am just playing around Raging Bull by Martin Scorsese and Battling Butler by Michel Keaton- someone from Charlie's days itself. They've all tried to plaster the sincerity of that job on screen. You can joke around, fool around as much as you like but that sport is respected with a jarring punch when the time comes. Not to say that Charlie doesn't respect it. His method of living up to that "dutiful" objective is somewhat different. Nay, not different. Mature. Ahead of time. He doesn't pay homage to those heavy lifters by putting them in the ring, but does it so elegantly in the dressing room. From hard work to the stakes that are played every night on the screen, everything is mocked or more accurately notified in Charlie's dictionary. There is a sense of pride in carrying that note. And maybe that is why he has crafted such an empathetic and a low key character in the rest of the screen time. He is wreck but an adoptable one. Another thing that makes this film incredibly different than the others is the jokes. All the jokes are an elaborative comic sketches that takes energy along with time for it to work, from you. And then there is the end of the tunnel in the City Lights. A purely unconditional and innocent act that penetrates your emotion as that good old symbol of love arrow does. The birds fly and sing by merrily.
